items.myth: Sigmoid is obsolete and should never appear in modern neural networks.
items.reality: Sigmoid is a poor default for deep hidden layers, but it remains standard for binary output probabilities, LSTM/GRU gates, and any layer that must emit values strictly in (0, 1).
items.example: A production churn model uses ReLU hidden layers but still applies σ on the final logit to return P(churn) ∈ (0, 1).

items.myth: Vanishing gradients only happen because sigmoid outputs are between 0 and 1.
items.reality: The mechanism is the shrinking derivative σ′(x)=σ(x)(1−σ(x)) in saturated regions, which backpropagation multiplies across layers. Tanh has a similar saturation problem despite zero-centered outputs.
items.example: At x=5, σ′(5)≈0.0067 regardless of whether you care about the 0–1 range — the slope is what kills deep stacks.

items.myth: If σ(0)=0.5, sigmoid hidden layers are zero-centered on average.
items.reality: σ(x) is always positive, so hidden activations are biased toward positive values. That asymmetry can slow learning compared to tanh or batch normalization.
items.example: Random inputs with mean 0 still yield σ(x)≈0.5 on average, but every individual activation is >0, not symmetric around 0.

items.myth: Sigmoid and logistic regression are unrelated concepts.
items.reality: They share the same function σ(x). Logistic regression applies σ to a linear score for classical classification; neural nets apply σ as a nonlinear activation. The function definition is canonical here; the ML workflow pairing lives in Logistic Regression.
items.example: ŷ=σ(wᵀx+b) in sklearn and σ(Wx+b) in a neural output layer use the identical squashing curve.
